study.journal: Williams GW, Berg NK, Reskallah A, Yuan X, Eltzschig HK. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: Contemporary Management and Novel Approaches during COVID-19. Anesthesiology. 2021 February 01;134(2):270-282.

population_context.population_description: Review of ARDS management strategies across multiple randomized controlled trials and observational studies involving critically ill patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome, including COVID-19 patients.
population_context.risk_group: Critically ill patients with ARDS, severe ARDS, COVID-19 related ARDS

intervention.name: Multiple ARDS management strategies
intervention.parameter_values: Small tidal volumes: 6 ml/kg PBW; Prone positioning: minimum 16 hours per day; Dexamethasone dosing as per individual trials; Conservative oxygen: PaO2 70-100 mm Hg or SpO2 88-92%
intervention.timing: During mechanical ventilation and ICU management

comparator.description: Various control arms across trials including conventional tidal volumes (12 ml/kg), lower PEEP levels, supine positioning, placebo, and liberal oxygenation (SpO2 >96%)

outcome_effect.outcome_name: Multiple outcomes including mortality, ventilator-free days, oxygenation, duration of mechanical ventilation

strengths: Comprehensive review of ARDS Network and PETAL Network trials with over 25 years of research collaboration
Synthesis of multiple high-quality randomized controlled trials
Inclusion of recent meta-analyses supporting prone positioning benefits
Discussion of contemporary evidence during COVID-19 pandemic
limitations: Review article synthesizing heterogeneous trial designs and populations
Some trials had methodological concerns including lack of blinding and crossover effects
Mixed results for PEEP optimization with potential bias from uncontrolled tidal volumes in some studies
Conservative oxygenation trial (LOCO2) terminated early, limiting full analysis
Limited sample sizes in some key steroid trials (n=8 control group in Meduri study)
adverse_events: Increased mortality at 28 days in conservative oxygen treatment group in LOCO2 trial
Five episodes of mesenteric ischemia in conservative oxygen treatment arm
Increased mortality when steroid treatment started after 14 days of ARDS diagnosis
Risk of endotracheal tube and vascular line displacement with prone positioning

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to critically ill ICU patients with ARDS across perioperative and critical care settings. Results are particularly relevant for COVID-19 related ARDS. Small tidal volume ventilation and prone positioning recommendations are strong for severe ARDS. Findings may not apply to mild and moderate ARDS for prone positioning due to underpowered trials.
applicability.subgroup_effects: Higher PEEP associated with improved survival in patients who objectively respond to increased PEEP (show improved oxygenation). Prone positioning efficacy demonstrated primarily in severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150). Early steroid administration (within 14 days) may be more beneficial than late administration.

quoted_sentences.design: Here, we describe select management strategies that have become foundations of ARDS clinical management and provide an update of emerging approaches for the treatment of ARDS related with COVID-19.
quoted_sentences.intervention: Amongst the best-established guidelines in managing ARDS patients is the use of small tidal volumes during mechanical ventilation. In 2000, Investigators from the ARDSNet Lower Tidal Volume Trial (ARMA) trial reported significantly decreased rates of mortality (31.0% vs. 39.8%) in ARDS patients ventilated with 6ml/kg of predicted body weight (kg) tidal volumes versus those with 12 ml/kg PBW.
quoted_sentences.results: In 2013, Guérin and colleagues reported the results of the Proning Severe ARDS Patients (PROSEVA) trial in which severe ARDS patients (PaO2/FiO2 less than 150 on FiO2 of at least 0.6) were randomized to prone positioning for a minimum of 16 hours a day. Patients randomized to prone positioning had a 50% reduction in mortality (16% versus 32.8%) at 28 days.
quoted_sentences.outcome: Despite these encouraging results in reducing mortality with the use of prone positioning, data from a large, multinational prospective observational study indicate that the maneuver was employed in only 16.3% of severe ARDS patients.

Author manuscript

Published in final edited form as:
Anesthesiology. 2021 February 01; 134(2): 270–282. doi:10.1097/ALN.0000000000003571.
Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: Contemporary
Management and Novel Approaches during COVID-19
George W. Williams, M.D.#1,*, Nathaniel K. Berg#1,*, Alexander Reskallah, M.D.1, Xiaoyi
Yuan, Ph.D.1, Holger K. Eltzschig, M.D., Ph.D.1
1Department of Anesthesiology, University of Texas Health Science Center at Houston, McGovern
Medical School, Houston, TX, USA
# These authors contributed equally to this work.
Abstract
Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) is defined as the rapid onset of non-cardiogenic
pulmonary edema resulting in respiratory failure and hypoxemia. Efforts over the past 25 years,
such as those of the ARDS and Prevention and Early Treatment of Acute Lung Injury (PETAL)
Networks, have demonstrated a praiseworthy collaboration to further optimize the management of
ARDS. However, improvements have been only moderate and ARDS remains a leading cause of
mortality in the perioperative and critical care setting. Recently, the significant morbidity and
mortality of ARDS have been emphasized by its high incidence in Coronavirus Disease 2019
(COVID-19) patients. A major hurdle to reducing ARDS mortality is that current treatment is
limited to preventive measures – such as the use of lung-protective ventilation. Therapeutic
approaches targeting the underlying inflammatory lung disease are areas of intensive research, but
have not been clinically implemented. Nevertheless, basic science and clinical research efforts that
are aimed at identifying novel treatment approaches and further improving outcomes for ARDS
are ongoing. Here, we review evidence-based management approaches for ARDS, while
highlighting those being investigated or heavily utilized in ARDS associated with COVID-19.
Summary Statement:
Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome remains a condition that carries a high mortality. Evidence-
based clinical management and emerging concepts for new therapies for COVID-19 are reviewed.
Introduction
The acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is defined as hypoxemia secondary to a
rapid onset of noncardiogenic pulmonary edema.1 Etiologic risk factors for ARDS
encompass both direct- and indirect-lung injuries including, but not limited to pneumonia,
sepsis, non-cardiogenic shock, aspiration, trauma, contusion, transfusion, and inhalation
